message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) is a novel coronavirus infection that has spread worldwide in a short period and caused a pandemic. The goal of this meta-analysis is to evaluate the prevalence of most common symptoms and complications of COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    All related studies assessing the clinical complications of COVID-19 have been identified through web search databases (PubMed and Scopus). Relevant data were extracted from these studies and analyzed by stata (ver 14) random-effects model. The heterogeneity of studies were assessed by
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>2</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    index. The publication bias was examined by Funnel plots and Egger’s test.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    30 studies were in our meta-analysis including 6 389 infected patients. The prevalence of most common symptoms were: fever 84.30% (95% CI: 77.13-90.37; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =97.74%), cough 63.01% (95% CI: 57.63-68.23; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =93.73%), dyspnea 37.16% (95% CI: 27.31-47.57%; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =98.32%), fatigue 34.22% (95% CI: 26.29-42.62; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =97.29%) and diarrhea 11.47 %(95% CI: 6.96-16.87; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =95.58%), respectively. The most prevalent complications were acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) 33.15% (95% CI: 23.35-43.73; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =98.56%), acute cardiac injury 13.77% (95% CI: 9.66-18.45; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =91.36%), arrhythmia 16.64% (95% CI: 9.34-25.5; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =92.29%), heart failure 11.50% (95% CI: 3.45-22.83; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =89.48%), and acute kidney injury (AKI) 8.40 %(95% CI: 5.15-12.31; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =95.22%, respectively. According to our analysis, mortality rate of COVID-19 patients were 12.29% (95% CI: 6.20-19.99; I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    =98.29%).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We assessed the prevalence of the main clinical complications of COVID-19 and found that after respiratory complications, cardiac and renal complications are the most common clinical complications of COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="simple">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>The most prevalent complication among critical cases of COVID-19 is ARDS.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>After pulmonary complications, cardiovascular complications (like arrhythmia, heart failure and acute cardiac injury) are the most important threats for COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>Renal complications (like AKI) happen as a result of COVID-19, but they are less prevalent than pulmonary and cardiovascular complications.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Critical Complications of COVID-19: A systematic Review and Meta-Analysis study
